title: Fooling polytopes
speaker: Li-Yang Tan
abstract: We give a pseudorandom generator that fools $m$-facet polytopes over $\{0,1\}^n$ with seed length $\mathrm{polylog}(m) \cdot \mathrm{log}(n)$. The previous best seed length had superlinear dependence on $m$. An immediate consequence is a deterministic quasipolynomial time algorithm for approximating the number of solutions to any $\{0,1\}$-integer program. Joint work with Ryan O'Donnell and Rocco Servedio.

proof_stages.label: Boolean-polytope PRG problem and theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines m-facet polytopes over the Boolean hypercube, equates discrepancy sets with PRGs, states an explicit PRG with seed length polylog(m)·poly(1/epsilon)·log n, and compares this with prior linear-in-m bounds and desired lower-bound behavior.

proof_stages.label: CLT route for a single regular halfspace
proof_stages.summary: For a regular linear form, Berry-Esseen gives CDF closeness to a Gaussian, and the Meka-Zuckerman bucket generator derandomizes this CLT so that a triangle-inequality argument fools all thresholds of the halfspace.

proof_stages.label: Multidimensional CLT route for regular intersections
proof_stages.summary: The regular-intersection result of Harsha, Klivans, and Meka is presented as a multidimensional analogue: regular rows of a matrix satisfy a CDF CLT, its pseudorandom version holds for the same bucketed generator, and hence regular polytopes are fooled.

proof_stages.label: General-halfspace reformulation beyond Gaussian limits
proof_stages.summary: Since nonregular halfspaces need not approach any Gaussian, the new result is reformulated as directly constructing a pseudorandom y such that Ax and Ay are close in multidimensional CDF distance, using a Lindeberg/hybrid strategy plus CNF-PRG and Littlewood-Offord ingredients.

proof_stages.label: Head-tail decomposition of arbitrary facets
proof_stages.summary: A regularity lemma for halfspaces decomposes each row into a small sparse head and a regular tail, but because the heads of many facets may cover all variables, the proof must exploit sparse heads rather than simply restrict them all.

proof_stages.label: Gaussian-smoothed orthant test functions
proof_stages.summary: The discontinuous orthant indicator defining CDF distance is replaced by a Gaussian-smoothed modifier that approximates the orthant outside a thin shell and has polylogarithmic bounds on sums of higher partial derivatives.

proof_stages.label: Hybrid replacement and Taylor/CNF control of the smooth test
proof_stages.summary: The smooth version is proved by swapping one bucket of uniform bits for limited-independent bits at a time, writing the bucket matrix as H+T, Taylor-expanding in the regular tail T, fooling sparse-head terms via CNF generators, and bounding Taylor remainders by the derivative estimates.

proof_stages.label: Littlewood-Offord anti-concentration transfer
proof_stages.summary: A high-dimensional Littlewood-Offord or surface-area bound shows that Ax and Ay put little mass in the smoothing shell near the polytope boundary, allowing the smooth orthant-fooling statement to imply the actual PRG/CDF statement.

examples.name: Polytope inside the unit cube

examples.engagement_reason: a polytope inside the unit cube is discussed only to note the measure issue and that Boolean points may miss it.

examples.name: Majority halfspace
examples.note: Majority, with all weights equal, is used as the canonical regular halfspace where no coordinate dominates and the Berry-Esseen picture is easiest to visualize.
examples.engagement_reason: majority is used as the canonical regular halfspace with equal weights, without a concrete computation on it.

examples.name: Dominant-coordinate or spiky halfspace
examples.note: The halfspace with one overwhelmingly large weight shows why ordinary CLT convergence fails without regularity and motivates isolating sparse head variables before treating the regular tail.
examples.engagement_reason: the dominant/spiky halfspace is analyzed to show CLT failure and to illustrate restricting the large-weight variables until a regular tail remains.

passes.reason: The one-dominant-coordinate spiky halfspace collapses essentially to a dictator and is the most non-regular extreme, used to show the CLT-style Gaussian approximation fails without regularity.
